facts_disclosed: Partner Lucía is human rights lawyer

turns.content: quick question. what are some good meals you can prep in advance that reheat well? I need stuff I can leave in the fridge and either of us can heat up whenever we get home

Great question! Meals that reheat well tend to be things with sauce or liquid that keeps them from drying out. Here are some solid options:

**Stews & soups:**
- Lentil soup — actually tastes better the next day
- Bean stew / chili — holds up beautifully for days
- Chicken or vegetable curry

**Grain-based:**
- Rice bowls with pre-cooked protein (the rice reheats fine if you sprinkle water on it before microwaving)
- Pasta bakes — lasagna, baked ziti, anything with sauce and cheese

**Latin staples that reheat perfectly:**
- Sancocho — almost better reheated
- Arroz con pollo
- Frijoles — they just get creamier

What kind of food do you both like? And when you say "whenever" — are we talking different schedules, late nights, unpredictable hours?

unpredictable is an understatement. some nights I get home at 8, some nights at midnight. and lucía is worse than me honestly. she's a human rights lawyer and her hours make mine look reasonable. we keep saying we'll cook together more and then one of us is still at work when the other finally eats

Two demanding careers with unpredictable hours — yeah, the "let's cook together" dream is sweet but the reality is more like whoever gets home first opens the fridge and hopes for the best.

Here's what works for couples in your situation:

**The Sunday cook-up:**
Pick one day (doesn't have to be Sunday) where you both have a couple of hours. Make 2-3 big batch meals and portion them into individual containers. Label them with the date. This gives you both a "choose your own dinner" situation all week without the guilt of ordering delivery every night.

**Top picks for your situation:**
- **Ajiaco** — make a huge pot, it keeps for 4-5 days and is basically a hug in bowl form when you get home late
- **A big tray of sudado** — fish or chicken, reheats in minutes
- **Grain bowls** — cook a batch of rice and a batch of quinoa, prep some roasted vegetables and a protein. Whoever gets home just assembles.
- **Empanada filling** — make the filling in advance, keep frozen empanada shells. Quick assembly, 15 minutes in the oven.

Does Lucía cook too, or would this mostly be on you?

lucía is a better cook than me which she loves to point out. when she actually has time she makes this incredible patacón pisao that I would commit crimes for. but her cases right now are brutal so she's been coming home wrecked. I want to make it so that when she walks in at 11pm there's something real waiting for her not just crackers and whatever cheese is left

That's a really thoughtful impulse. Coming home at 11pm to a real meal someone prepared for you — that's love in practical form, especially when you're emotionally drained from the kind of work she does.

Okay, here's a game plan built around the idea of "Valentina's midnight kitchen":

**Meals that are perfect at 11pm (not too heavy, still nourishing):**

1. **Crema de ahuyama** (butternut squash soup) — blend it smooth, leave it in a pot. She just turns on the stove for 5 minutes. Add a piece of bread and it's comfort food without weighing her down before sleep.

2. **Chicken and rice soup (caldo)** — light, warm, easy on the stomach. Make a big batch early in the week.

3. **Pre-made wraps in the fridge** — tortilla, beans, avocado, shredded chicken, salsa. She grabs one, heats it or eats it cold. Five seconds of effort.

4. **A thermos system** — this sounds old-fashioned but: make soup or caldo, put it in a good thermos before you go to bed. It stays warm for hours. She walks in, it's already the right temperature.

Also — and this is small but it matters — leave a note with the food. Even just "heat for 3 min, I love you" on a sticky note on the container. When someone gets home at 11pm from a hard day, that little thing means everything.

What kinds of flavors does she gravitate toward?

she's from the coast originally so she loves seafood. coconut rice. anything with ají. she puts ají on everything it's honestly alarming. the thermos idea is genius actually because sometimes she doesn't even want to turn on a stove she just wants to eat and collapse

A costeña who puts ají on everything — I love that. Okay, tailored to her palate:

**Seafood-friendly meal prep:**

1. **Arroz con coco + fish** — make the coconut rice in a big batch (it reheats perfectly, just add a splash of coconut milk when warming). Prep a simple fish — even canned tuna dressed up with lime, ají, onion, cilantro works great when she's too tired for real cooking.

2. **Cazuela de mariscos** — if you can get good shrimp and fish, make a big pot with coconut milk base. It's rich, it's coastal, it reheats like a dream. Freeze portions for the really bad weeks.

3. **Ceviche-style bowl** — prepped in the fridge, served cold, no heating required at all. Shrimp, lime, red onion, cilantro, ají (obviously). She just grabs the container.

4. **Coconut lentil soup with a heavy ají hit** — this is the thermos champion. Thick, hearty, coastal flavors, and the spice warms you up.

**For the ají situation:**
Make a big jar of homemade ají — lime juice, cilantro, habanero or ají amarillo, salt. Keep it in the fridge. She puts it on whatever you make. That way even a simple rice bowl becomes "her" food.

The thermos + homemade ají combo means she walks in, pours, adds her ají, eats, done. Two minutes from door to dinner.

Want me to give you a specific recipe for any of these?
give me the cazuela de mariscos one. I've never made it but I've eaten it a million times in cartagena. if I can make her a good cazuela she will literally propose to me on the spot

Ha! High stakes cooking. Let's make sure this cazuela is proposal-worthy.

**Cazuela de Mariscos (serves 4-6, stores perfectly for 3-4 days)**

**Ingredients:**
- 500g shrimp (shell-on preferred for stock, peel before adding)
- 300g white fish (mojarra, robalo, or whatever's fresh), cut in chunks
- 200g squid rings (optional but adds great texture)
- 1 can coconut milk (400ml)
- 2 tablespoons tomato paste
- 1 large onion, diced
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 red bell pepper, diced
- 1 large tomato, chopped
- Juice of 2 limes
- Handful of fresh cilantro
- 1 teaspoon cumin
- Salt, pepper, ají to taste
- 2 tablespoons oil or butter
- 1 cup fish or shrimp stock (or water + a bouillon cube)

**Steps:**

1. If using shell-on shrimp, peel them and boil the shells in 1 cup water for 10 minutes to make a quick stock. Strain and save that liquid — it's liquid gold.

2. In a big pot, heat oil. Sauté onion until soft (5 min), then add garlic, bell pepper, and tomato. Cook until everything breaks down, about 8 minutes.

3. Add tomato paste and cumin, stir for a minute until fragrant.

4. Pour in the coconut milk and the shrimp stock. Stir well. Let it simmer for 10-15 minutes until slightly thickened.

5. Add the fish chunks first (they take longer). Cook 5 minutes.

6. Add shrimp and squid. Cook just 3-4 minutes — shrimp is done when pink, don't overcook or they get rubbery.

7. Squeeze in the lime juice, add cilantro, adjust salt.

**For reheating:** Low heat on the stove, add a splash of coconut milk if it's thickened too much. Microwave works too but stove is better for seafood.

**Serve with:** Coconut rice and patacones if you're going full coastal. Or just with white rice and her ají.

**Pro tip for the proposal:** Serve it in a deep bowl, not a plate. Cazuela is meant to be eaten from something you can hold. And have arroz con coco ready on the side.

Good luck. Report back on whether she proposes.

this is PERFECT. I'm screenshotting this entire conversation. I'm going to attempt this saturday when we both theoretically have the day off which means lucía will probably still be working until 3 but at least the cazuela will be there when she surfaces

The cazuela will wait for her. That's the beauty of a good stew — it just sits there getting better while everyone else is busy living their lives.

A few last tips for Saturday:

- Get the freshest seafood you can find. If you're in Bogotá, hit the market early.
- Don't skip the shrimp shell stock step. That's the difference between "good" and "where did you learn to cook this."
- Make the ají jar fresh that morning too — it'll be better than anything from a bottle.
- Double the recipe. Future-you will thank present-you when it's Tuesday night and there's cazuela in the fridge.

And honestly, the fact that you're doing this not as a grand gesture but because you want her to walk in at 11pm and have something real waiting — that's better than any restaurant. She's lucky. You both are.

Enjoy the cooking. Let me know if you need any other recipes for the rotation.
